sentences.base_language: I take the subway in Seoul.
sentences.grammarNote: 타다 (tada) means 'to ride / to take' a vehicle: 지하철을 타다 (take the subway), 버스를 타다 (take the bus), 택시를 타다 (take a taxi). The vehicle is the object marked with 을/를.

sentences.base_language: Seoul's subway system is very convenient.
sentences.grammarNote: 편리하다 (pyeollihada) = to be convenient — Sino-Korean: 편리 (便利, convenient). 은 = topic marker after a consonant: 지하철은 frames the subway as the topic being commented on. 정말 (jeongmal) = really/truly — a very common intensifier in everyday speech.

sentences.base_language: I look at the route map.
sentences.grammarNote: 노선도 (nosendo) = route map — 노선 (路線, route/line) + 도 (圖, map/diagram). 를 = object marker after a vowel: 노선도를. 보다 (boda) = to see/look/watch — one of the most versatile verbs in Korean, used for maps, TV, people, and more.

sentences.base_language: I don't know which line to take.
sentences.grammarNote: -(으)ㄴ/는지 모르다 is the pattern for 'not knowing whether/which/how': 어디인지 몰라요 (I don't know where), 어떻게 하는지 몰라요 (I don't know how to do it). The -지 clause embeds an indirect question.

sentences.base_language: Excuse me, how do I get to Gyeongbokgung?
sentences.grammarNote: 저기요 (jeogiyo) = 'excuse me' — used to get a stranger's attention on the street or in a shop. 어떻게 (eotteoke) = 'how' — the adverb form of 어떻다 (to be how). 에 = destination marker: 경복궁에 (to Gyeongbokgung). This is a model phrase for asking for directions.

sentences.base_language: Take line three and get off at Gyeongbokgung Station.
sentences.grammarNote: 내리다 (naerida) means 'to get off' (a vehicle). Its partner is 타다 (to get on/ride). Together they cover boarding and disembarking: 지하철을 타다 (get on the subway), 지하철에서 내리다 (get off the subway).

감사합니다 (gamsahamnida) = formal 'thank you' — 감사 (感謝, gratitude) + 합니다 (formal ending). The polite everyday form is 감사해요. The more casual form is 고마워요. Use 감사합니다 with strangers or in formal situations; 고마워요 with friends.

출구 (chulgu) = exit — Sino-Korean: 출 (出, exit) + 구 (口, opening/mouth). Its opposite is 입구 (ipgu) = entrance — 입 (入, enter) + 구 (口). 맞는 = 'correct/matching' — the present modifier of 맞다 (to be correct/fit). 찾다 (chajda) = to find/look for.

sentences.base_language: I exit at exit number five.
sentences.grammarNote: 번 (beon) is the counter for numbers/ordinals: 1번 (number 1), 5번 출구 (exit number 5). It attaches to Sino-Korean numbers (일, 이, 삼...). Compare with the native Korean counter 번째 which means 'nth in order'.

목적지 (mokjeokji) = destination — Sino-Korean: 목적 (目的, purpose/goal) + 지 (地, place). 도착하다 (dochakhada) = to arrive — 도착 (到着, arrival) + 하다. 에 after a place = 'at/to': 목적지에 도착해요 = 'arrive at the destination'. Opposite: 출발하다 (chulbalhada) = to depart.
